_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve is a graph showing yields on U.S. Treasury securities across maturities from 1 month to 30 years; its slope reflects market expectations. A normal upward slope means long-term yields, like the 10-year Treasury yield at 4.57%, are higher than short-term yields, compensating for risk and inflation over time. During 2022-2024, the 10-year to 2-year (10Y-2Y) Treasury spread was inverted, meaning short-term yields exceeded long-term yields, signaling economic caution. Currently, the 10Y-2Y spread is 0.37 points, indicating the yield curve has re-steepened above zero after the deepest inversion at 0.0 points.
notes.signal: The 10Y-2Y spread is a closely watched recession indicator, as inversions (spread below zero) historically precede recessions by 6-18 months. The curve spent 2022-2024 inverted but has since un-inverted, shown by the positive 0.37 spread today, illustrating a flattening then re-steepening process. The New York Fed's recession probability estimate stands at 31%, reflecting moderate recession risk aligned with an un-inverting yield curve. However, the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before recessions, so an un-inverted curve does not rule out future economic downturns.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve’s positive 10Y-2Y spread of 0.37 and 10-year Treasury yield at 4.57% suggest the market no longer signals an immediate recession. The NY Fed’s 31% recession probability indicates moderate risk but not certainty. Together, these metrics imply cautious optimism without indicating precise timing of economic shifts.
